mappings.rationale: Covers the history of visual arts including sculpture, with references to sculptors like Ghiberti, directly relevant to Western sculpture history (C.1).
Addresses sculpture explicitly as a keyword, along with aesthetics, significant form, and representation -- central to elements of design and iconography (A.1, A.2).
Covers modern sculptors like Tatlin and Oldenburg, relevant to nonrepresentational sculpture (B.2.b) and Western sculpture history (C.1).
Covers contemporary sculptural practices including installation and environmental sculpture (B.1) and the global art market for sculpture.
Covers Renaissance sculptors including Ghiberti, Alberti, and da Vinci, a major period in Western sculpture history (C.1).
Egyptian sculpture including devotional images, portraits, and commemorative monuments spans representational sculpture (B.2.a) and ceremonial objects (B.3.a).
Dada and Surrealist sculptors challenged traditional sculptural forms, relevant to nonrepresentational sculpture (B.2.b) and Western sculpture history (C.1).
Avant-garde movements expanded the definition and materials of sculpture, relevant to kinetic and environmental sculpture (B.1) and nonrepresentational sculpture (B.2.b).
Christian sculpture includes devotional images, commemorative monuments, and ritualistic objects -- directly relevant to B.2.a, B.3.a, and B.3.c.
Classical Greek and Roman sculpture including temple sculpture at Bassae is foundational to Western sculpture history, covering human figures and devotional images (B.2.a, C.1).
Mythological subjects were central to classical sculptural iconography, relevant to the iconography of sculpture (A.2) and representational sculpture (B.2.a).
Futurist sculpture explored kinetic forms and modern materials, directly relevant to kinetic sculpture (B.1) and Western sculpture history (C.1).
Etruscan sarcophagi and bronze figures represent a significant sculptural tradition in Western sculpture history (C.1).
Covers prehistoric figurines and sculptural objects, relevant to primitive and folk sculpture (B.4).
Covers rock art and prehistoric sculptural heritage, relevant to understanding ancient and primitive sculpture in its archaeological context (B.4, C.1).
Covers properties of stone, metal, clay, and glass — sculptural materials explicitly listed in A.3 (the materials of sculpture).
